name: Australia
summary: English-native study→485→skilled/PR route with dual citizenship retained (Australia is on Bangladesh's Dual Nationality Certificate list) and unrestricted spouse work during a coursework master's; tempered by Bangladesh's January 2026 move to student-visa Evidence Level 3 (strictest), high independent-PR points for ICT, and a sharp post-Bondi (Dec 2025) Islamophobia surge.

links.title: Department of Home Affairs — Immigration and citizenship (official)
Study Australia (Australian Government)

job-market.summary: Deep English-language tech market across Sydney/Melbourne with strong pay and an AI premium, though software eased off the 2025 shortage list.

pros.text: Tech pay is high: ABS ICT-professional median ~AUD 119,600/yr (Aug 2025) and Information-Media/Telecom AWOTE ~AUD 139,600/yr (Nov 2025), with an AI wage premium.
Software Engineer (ANZSCO 261313) remains on the Core Skills Occupation List, keeping employer sponsorship (subclass 482) open.
Two deep tech hubs (Sydney, Melbourne) plus Brisbane/Perth absorb both engineers; English-language market suits Bangladeshi graduates.

cons.text: Software developer/engineer left the JSA 2025 Occupation Shortage List in all states; overall ICT shortage fell ~49% over 2024–25, so no across-the-board shortage tailwind.
A softer 2026 entry-level market caps quick placement for both spouses.

links.title: Jobs and Skills Australia — 2025 Occupation Shortage List Key Findings (PDF, official)
ABS — Average Weekly Earnings, Australia (Nov 2025)

visa-access.summary: Bangladesh sits at Evidence Level 3 (strictest) from 9 January 2026, raising documentation, funds proof and refusal risk, though lodgement and biometrics stay in Dhaka.

pros.text: Applications are lodged online with VFS biometrics in Dhaka — no travel to New Delhi required.
The Genuine Student requirement (Direction 106, since 23 Mar 2024) is a structured questionnaire rather than an open-ended interview.

cons.text: Out-of-cycle re-rating moved Bangladesh from Evidence Level 2 to Level 3 (strictest) on 9 January 2026, citing a Nov–Dec 2025 spike in forged bank guarantees and fake transcripts; case officers must now manually verify records.
EL3 demands 3+ months of consistent bank statements, detailed source-of-funds and authenticated transcripts; processing has lengthened from roughly 3 to 8 weeks.
Living-cost proof is AUD 29,710 (primary) plus AUD 10,394 (spouse) and AUD 4,449 per child (since 10 May 2024).

links.title: Home Affairs — Student visa (subclass 500) (official)
Home Affairs — Financial capacity requirement update (AUD 29,710), official
The Daily Star — Australia tightens visa scrutiny for Bangladeshi students (Jan 2026)

citizenship.summary: Four years' lawful residence (study/work time counts, only the last 12 months as PR), a modest English-only test, dual citizenship retained, and a top-tier passport.

pros.text: Dual citizenship works both ways: Australia has permitted it since 2002, and Australia is on Bangladesh's Dual Nationality Certificate list, so a naturalising Bangladeshi keeps BD nationality.
Four years' lawful residence (student and work time count) with only the final 12 months required as a permanent resident.
Australian passport ranks equal 7th on the Henley 2026 index (~182 destinations).

cons.text: The citizenship test is English-only (20 questions, 5/5 on Australian values plus ≥75% overall).
Reaching citizenship still depends on first obtaining PR, which is the binding gate (scored under PR Pathway).

links.title: Home Affairs — Citizenship for permanent residents (official)
Home Affairs — Citizenship test and interview (official)

post-study-work.summary: Temporary Graduate (subclass 485) gives a coursework master's 2–3 years of full-work-rights runway, and both graduate spouses can each hold their own 485.

pros.text: Post-Higher Education Work stream: usually 2–3 years for a coursework master's, with unrestricted in-country work in any role.
Both 2022 graduates are under 35, so each can independently hold their own 485.

cons.text: The stream age limit was cut to 35 or under (1 Mar 2026 update, down from 50) and a second 485 is now tied to shortage lists — adverse since software left the shortage list.
Conversion to long-term skilled/PR is not automatic; it depends on an employer sponsor or competitive points.

links.title: Home Affairs — Temporary Graduate (subclass 485) Post-Higher Education Work (official)
Study Australia — Temporary Graduate visa (subclass 485)

spouse-family.summary: The dependant partner of a coursework master's student may work more than 48 hours a fortnight (effectively unrestricted), and full partner work continues on the 485 and skilled/PR visas.

pros.text: A partner of a coursework-master's (or master's Extended) student can work more than 48 hours a fortnight, escaping the cap that binds bachelor-level dependants.
Partner and unmarried children under 18 can be included on the student application; full partner work continues on the 485 and skilled/PR visas.

cons.text: The partner cannot start work until the student's course has commenced (proof of enrolment required).
Dependent-child schooling must be budgeted (annual school-cost evidence ~AUD 13,502) until the family reaches PR.

links.title: Home Affairs — Student visa (subclass 500): including family members & work hours (official)
Home Affairs — Working hours for student visa holders (official)

msc-study.summary: Broad English-taught CS/AI master's with accessible admission for a BD BSc-CSE graduate, but international CS/AI tuition runs high (~AUD 44k/yr).

pros.text: Wide, well-ranked English-taught CS/AI catalogue; a standard coursework master's qualifies for the 485 post-study runway.
Admission is attainable for a BD BSc-CSE graduate (e.g. RMIT Master of AI: bachelor in computing/engineering at GPA ~2.0/4.0 plus IELTS 6.5).
Students may work up to 48 hours a fortnight in session (unlimited in breaks); the 2026 national intake rose to 295,000.

cons.text: International CS/AI tuition is high — e.g. RMIT Master of AI ~AUD 44,160/yr (~AUD 88,320 over two years) — with limited scholarships for international master's students.

links.title: RMIT — Master of Artificial Intelligence (MC271) tuition & entry (2026)
Study Australia — Increased student intake for 2026 (295,000)

pr-pathway.summary: PR exists via employer-sponsored 482→186 (~2 years) or competitive points-based GSM, but ICT independent invitations ran at 85–100 points in late 2025.

pros.text: Skills in Demand (subclass 482) → Employer Nomination (subclass 186) offers a clearer employer-sponsored PR after ~2 years; software engineer is CSOL-eligible.
PR language requirement is only Competent English (achievable for two engineers).

cons.text: Independent GSM (subclass 189) is highly competitive for ICT: the 13 Nov 2025 round invited Software Engineers at ~85–100 points (Tier 3).
Employer-sponsored PR needs a willing sponsor and a CSOL-aligned role; the permanent program remains capped.

links.title: Home Affairs — SkillSelect invitation rounds (official)
Home Affairs — Skills in Demand visa (subclass 482) (official)

income-cost.summary: Two tech salaries leave a comfortable surplus, but Sydney/Melbourne housing is among the most expensive globally and rents are still rising.

pros.text: A dual tech household (~AUD 240k–280k gross) supports strong savings and easy remittance to Bangladesh.

cons.text: Housing is costly: Sydney median dwelling value ~AUD 1.30m and Melbourne ~AUD 0.83m (Mar 2026); NSW median rent ~AUD 650/wk, VIC ~AUD 520/wk.
National rents rose ~5.7% year-on-year to March 2026, squeezing the budget — especially during the student phase under the 48-hour cap.

links.title: ABS — Latest insights into the rental market
Cotality — Home Value Index (April 2026, PDF)

healthcare.summary: Excellent Medicare for PRs/citizens, but Bangladesh has no reciprocal agreement, so the couple carries mandatory OSHC/private cover through the whole temporary phase.

pros.text: Medicare gives permanent residents and citizens high-quality, low-cost universal care plus a strong welfare safety net.

cons.text: Bangladesh is not among the reciprocal-health-care-agreement countries, so there is no Medicare access until PR.
OSHC is mandatory for the whole student stay (students and dependants) and adequate cover is a 485 condition; it excludes dental, optical and physiotherapy.

links.title: Services Australia — Reciprocal Health Care Agreements (official)
Study Australia — Overseas Student Health Cover (OSHC)

culture-language.summary: English-native and highly multicultural with no language barrier for status, though social-cohesion data and post-Bondi tension temper acceptance for some minorities.

pros.text: English is the working and everyday language, so there is no separate language test friction for work, PR or citizenship.
Highly multicultural with large established South-Asian communities and high livability.

cons.text: The 2025 Mapping Social Cohesion report finds persistent prejudice toward some faith/immigrant groups and lower belonging among non-English-speaking-background migrants.
Social tension rose sharply after the December 2025 Bondi attack.

links.title: Home Affairs — English language requirements (official)
Scanlon Institute — Mapping Social Cohesion 2025

safety-law.summary: Top-tier rule of law (WJP 2025: 11th, 0.80) and generally low everyday crime, but the Dec 2025 Bondi attack drove a steep rise in anti-minority incidents.

pros.text: WJP Rule of Law Index 2025: 11th of 143 (score 0.80); Global Peace Index 2025: 18th of 163 — structurally safe with stable courts.
Federal anti-discrimination protections apply to migrants and minorities.

cons.text: Post-Bondi (Dec 2025) anti-Muslim incidents surged (Special Envoy: +740% since the attack), with mosque and community vandalism.
WJP score slipped two ranks on checks-and-balances and judicial limits on government power.

links.title: World Justice Project — Rule of Law Index 2025 (Australia)
Global Peace Index 2025 (PDF)

politics.summary: Stable Labor majority (re-elected May 2025) but a sustained 2024–26 migration crackdown and post-Bondi climate make policy volatile for newcomers.

pros.text: Very stable institutions: Labor won a landslide second term (3 May 2025, ~94 House seats).
Skilled migration remains operative (CSOL, Skills in Demand) and the 2026 student intake rose to 295,000.

cons.text: Continual restrictions across stages (EL3 for Bangladesh, doubled 485 fee, second-485 shortage-tie, high ICT points) make policy volatile for incoming migrants.
EL3 specifically targets South-Asian markets, and PM Albanese signalled in June 2026 a push to cut net migration toward 225,000.

links.title: 2025 Australian federal election (overview)
Study Australia — Increased student intake for 2026

tax.summary: Progressive 2025–26 resident tax to 45% plus a 2% Medicare levy, no expat relief, and no Australia–Bangladesh double-tax treaty.

pros.text: Individual (not joint) assessment with an AUD 18,200 tax-free threshold; no employee social-security contributions beyond the 2% Medicare levy.

cons.text: Resident brackets reach 45% above AUD 190,000, plus a 2% Medicare levy (~47% top effective rate); no special expat/foreign-worker tax regime.
Australia has no income-tax treaty with Bangladesh (Treasury lists 47 partners; Bangladesh is absent), so no treaty relief on repatriation.

links.title: ATO — Tax rates for Australian residents (2025–26)
Australian Treasury — Income tax treaties

muslim-diaspora.summary: Large established Bangladeshi-Muslim community with abundant mosques and halal food, but the post-Bondi Islamophobia surge has sharply worsened day-to-day sentiment.

pros.text: ~51,500 Bangladesh-born residents (84% Muslim) and ~813,000 Muslims (3.2%, 2021 Census), concentrated in Sydney and Melbourne.
Mosques (Lakemba is the largest) and halal food are widely available, alongside a rich South-Asian cultural ecosystem.

cons.text: After the 14 December 2025 Bondi attack, anti-Muslim incidents surged (Special Envoy: +740%; ANIC reported a near-tripling), including mosque and cemetery vandalism and threats — especially toward women in hijab.

links.title: ABS — 2021 Census religious diversity (official)
Islamophobia surge since the Bondi attack (report)

direct-work-route.summary: A complete, well-documented direct-work chain for BD ICT engineers. Software Engineer (ANZSCO 261313) is on the Core Skills Occupation List; the Skills in Demand (482) Core Skills stream is open offshore (income threshold A$73,150, easily cleared by software pay) so an employer can sponsor and relocate directly from Dhaka — though the alternative points-tested Skilled Independent (189) route is intensely competitive for ICT (~90+ points). 482 → Employer Nomination Scheme 186 PR after 2 years (Temporary Residence Transition), or 189 = direct PR on grant → citizenship at 4 years, with dual citizenship retained (Australia is on Bangladesh's DNC list). Established, fast-growing BD community (~51,500 Bangladesh-born, 67% naturalised). Friction: ACS skills assessment, the Skilling Australians Fund levy (a small-employer deterrent), and a flat 2025–26 program.

pros.text: Complete direct-work chain: offshore-eligible 482 on an open ICT occupation list, employer-sponsored from Dhaka, 186 PR after 2 years (or 189 direct PR), citizenship at 4 years, dual retained (Australia on Bangladesh's DNC list).
Established, fast-growing Bangladeshi community (~51,500 born, 67% citizens) shows the route works; software-engineer salaries clear the A$73,150 threshold easily.

cons.text: The no-sponsor 189 points route is intensely competitive for ICT (~90+ points); a skills assessment via ACS is required.
The Skilling Australians Fund levy (small business ~A$1,200/yr per worker, not passable to the worker) deters small employers; the 2025–26 program is flat (185,000).
